# Scanned Reports and OCR Quality Gates {#sec-chapter-06}

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Recognise when a DDR PDF has no extractable text and needs OCR.
- Run OCR on a scanned page and understand why the raw output is often
  too noisy to index as-is.
- Build a quality gate that rejects bad OCR output *before* it enters your
  search index, rather than trying to fix it after the fact.

## Operational problem

Chapter 1's `extract_text()` handled the case where `page.extract_text()`
returns `None` by substituting an empty string and moving on — a
reasonable choice for a five-file teaching example, but a real archive
will contain faxed reports, badly scanned pages, and rotated images where
that "empty string" is actually hiding real operational content. Worse:
OCR on a low-quality scan can produce text that *looks* plausible but is
substantially garbled — and if that garbled text makes it into your search
index, every downstream chapter inherits the corruption silently.

## Example DDR extract

::: {.callout-note title="OCR output before and after quality gating"}
```
Clean digital extraction:
  "0600-1130  POOH FR 9,842' TO CHANGE BHA."

Degraded OCR output from a poor scan:
  "O6OO-1l3O POO+I FR 9,84Z' T0 CHANGE B+IA -- -- -- -- --"
```
The second block is exactly the kind of text a naive pipeline would embed
and index without complaint — and exactly the kind of text a quality gate
should catch and route to a review queue instead.
:::

## Theory

Getting OCR to run is the easy part — `pytesseract`, cloud OCR APIs, and
PDF-rendering libraries all do a competent job of turning a page image
into text. The hard part, and the one that actually determines whether
your downstream system is trustworthy, is deciding **whether to trust the
output at all**. A quality gate scores OCR text against a handful of cheap
heuristics — is there enough text, is the alphabetic-to-symbol ratio
sane, is there suspicious repeated garbage — and rejects anything that
fails, routing it to manual review instead of silently indexing noise.

This chapter's companion repository, [DDR_RAG_Pipeline](https://github.com/djimrastephane/DDR_RAG_Pipeline),
implements exactly this pattern in `src/rag_pdf/ocr_quality.py`. Its
`evaluate_ocr_quality()` function checks four independent flags —
`low_text_density`, `high_symbol_ratio`, `repeated_garbage`,
`low_line_count` — and rejects a page only once **two or more** flags
fire, so no single noisy-but-legitimate page (e.g. a short report with a
lot of numeric data) gets discarded on one false positive.

## Implementation

Build a simplified version of the same idea:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# code/chapter_06/ocr_quality_gate.py
import re
from collections import Counter

def evaluate_ocr_quality(text: str, min_chars: int = 200,
                          min_alpha_words: int = 30,
                          max_symbol_ratio: float = 0.35,
                          reject_min_flags: int = 2) -> dict:
    alpha_tokens = re.findall(r"[A-Za-z]+", text)
    non_ws = [c for c in text if not c.isspace()]
    noisy = sum(1 for c in non_ws if not c.isalnum() and c not in set("%.,()/-:'"))
    symbol_ratio = noisy / max(len(non_ws), 1)

    flags = {
        "low_text_density": len(text) < min_chars or len(alpha_tokens) < min_alpha_words,
        "high_symbol_ratio": symbol_ratio > max_symbol_ratio,
    }
    active = [k for k, v in flags.items() if v]
    return {"reject_ocr": len(active) >= reject_min_flags, "flags": flags}
```

The full production version — including the repeated-garbage detector and
line-count check — is in the companion repository at
`src/rag_pdf/ocr_quality.py`, alongside `src/rag_pdf/rotation_handler.py`
for detecting and correcting upside-down or sideways scans, and
`src/rag_pdf/table_ocr_handoff.py` for the case where a *table* (not
narrative text) needs OCR fallback.

## Practical exercise

**Try it yourself:** Feed both the clean and degraded example strings
above through `evaluate_ocr_quality()`. Confirm the clean text passes and
the degraded text is rejected.

**You'll know it worked when:** you can explain, from the flags returned,
*why* the degraded text failed — not just that it did.

## Challenge exercise

**Challenge:** Clone the companion repository and read
`src/rag_pdf/ocr_quality.py` in full. Identify one flag the production
version has that the simplified version above doesn't, and explain what
failure mode it catches that the simplified version would miss.

## Key takeaways

- OCR quality gating is cheap, heuristic, and non-negotiable — an
  unfiltered noisy page corrupts every downstream chapter's retrieval.
- Require multiple independent signals to agree before rejecting a page;
  a single heuristic alone produces too many false positives.
- Reject-and-flag-for-review beats silently-index-anyway every time
  traceability matters more than completeness.

## Repository files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `code/chapter_06/ocr_quality_gate.py` | Simplified OCR quality gate |
| `DDR_RAG_Pipeline/src/rag_pdf/ocr_quality.py` | Production quality gate (companion repo) |
| `DDR_RAG_Pipeline/src/rag_pdf/rotation_handler.py` | Scan rotation detection (companion repo) |

## Suggested next step

Once you trust the text coming out of OCR and digital extraction alike,
the next question is how to break it into pieces small enough to embed
and retrieve — without cutting a stuck-pipe narrative in half across two
chunks. That's Chapter 7.
